messages.content:
### Summary: *Social Enterprise in Central and Eastern Europe*

This text serves as the introduction and comprehensive overview of social enterprise (SE) within the Central and Eastern European (CEE) region. It is the fourth volume in a series produced by the **International Comparative Social Enterprise Models (ICSEM) Project**.

#### Key Points:

*   **Research Approach:** The ICSEM Project rejects the pursuit of a single, encompassing global definition for social enterprise. Instead, it utilizes a "bottom-up" strategy to capture the immense diversity of SEs within their specific local and national contexts. This provides legitimacy to embedded institutional models while allowing for international comparison.
*   **Defining Social Enterprise:** Social enterprises are characterized by a combination of entrepreneurial behavior and a primary focus on social or societal aims. They typically blend market income from the sale of goods/services with other resource streams to foster social innovation—the implementation of new ideas to drive society toward fairer and more sustainable outcomes [S.3].
*   **Regional Context:** The book documents the emergence and development of SEs across various CEE countries, including Bulgaria, Croatia, the Czech Republic, Georgia, Hungary, Latvia, Lithuania, Poland, Romania, Russia, and the Slovak Republic [S.6–S.7].
*   **Core Research Themes:**
    *   The influence of historical and institutional legacies on current SE models [S.7].
    *   The significance of external financing and ecosystem development [S.8].
    *   The role of rural SEs and the interaction between endogenous and exogenous drivers [S.8].
*   **Collaboration:** The publication is a result of an eight-year partnership (2013–2020) involving approximately 230 researchers across 55 countries, supported by the EMES International Research Network and the COST Action "Empower-SE" [S.9].

#### Educational Relevance:
This text acts as a foundational reference for students and researchers, offering both national mapping of social enterprises and cross-country comparative analyses to help define the state of the art in the field [S.2, S.3].

Lesson 3: Social Enterprise in Central and Eastern Europe - Presentation5 slides
Social Enterprise in Central and Eastern Europe
  • Explores social enterprise development in the CEE region.
  • Part of the global ICSEM Project research series.

The provided text outlines the scope, methodology, and objectives of the ICSEM Project’s research into social enterprise, specifically focusing on the volume titled *Social Enterprise in Central and Eastern Europe*.

**Research Methodology and Philosophy**
The ICSEM Project adopted a "bottom-up" approach to research rather than attempting to enforce a singular, restrictive definition of social enterprise [S.2]. Recognizing the "impossibility of a unified definition," the project sought to document the immense diversity of the field across local and national contexts. This strategy allowed the researchers to validate locally embedded initiatives while simultaneously identifying broader, common international models [S.2].

**Defining Social Enterprise and Social Innovation**
The work defines social enterprises as organizations that integrate entrepreneurial spirit and behavior with a primary focus on achieving social or societal goals [S.3]. These entities typically sustain themselves through a combination of market-generated income and other resource streams [S.3]. Furthermore, the text defines social innovation as the implementation of new ideas or initiatives designed to transition society toward a more sustainable and equitable state [S.3].

**Aim and Scope of the Routledge Series**
The series, titled *Routledge Studies in Social Enterprise & Social Innovation*, explores the evolving research frontiers of the field [S.3]. It emphasizes the study of participatory governance and the dynamics of social innovation within civil society organizations, the third sector, and public-private partnerships [S.3].

**Academic and Professional Significance**
The book *Social Enterprise in Central and Eastern Europe* serves as a key reference for academics, policymakers, and practitioners [S.2]. It was developed as part of the "Empower-SE" COST Action, which supports research networks and emphasizes the importance of cross-European collaboration in shaping the next generation of social enterprise scholarship [S.4]. The publication is available in Open Access format, ensuring broad reach for its findings on emerging social entrepreneurship trends in the region [S.5].

This document outlines the contents of a scholarly collection examining social enterprise (SE) models across Central and Eastern Europe (CEE). The book is structured into two main parts and focuses on the institutional, historical, and economic factors influencing the sector.

**Part I: National Overviews**
This section provides a detailed analysis of the SE landscape in 11 specific countries: Bulgaria, Croatia, the Czech Republic, Georgia, Hungary, Latvia, Lithuania, Poland, Romania, Russia, and the Slovak Republic (pp. [S.6]-[S.7]). Each chapter addresses the transition from historical and institutional roots to contemporary developments within the respective national socioeconomic contexts (pp. [S.6]-[S.7]).

**Part II: Comparative Analyses**
This section transcends individual country narratives to synthesize broader regional trends. Key themes include:

- Institutional Legacies: An investigation into how historical foundations continue to shape current SE practices in countries like Poland, Hungary, the Czech Republic, and Croatia (p. [S.7]).
- Financial Dynamics: An evaluation of the impact of external funding on the trajectory of social entrepreneurship (p. [S.8]).
- Development Drivers: A study contrasting endogenous factors versus exogenous influences in sectoral growth (p. [S.8]).
- Geographical Specificity: A comparative look at the challenges and characteristics of SEs operating specifically within rural Baltic landscapes (p. [S.8]).
- Theoretical Testing: An effort to validate the relevance and applicability of established global SE models within the unique CEE environment (p. [S.8]).

The volume serves as a comprehensive mapping of the plurality of SE models, utilizing comparative research to understand how these organizations reconcile their social missions with economic sustainability (pp. [S.6]-[S.8]).

### Summary of the Preface and Acknowledgements (ICSEM Project)

**Project Overview and Scope**
The ICSEM (International Comparative Social Enterprise Models) Project is a comprehensive research initiative spanning eight years. It investigates the diverse models and ecosystems of social enterprises across approximately 55 countries. The project output includes a four-volume book series covering Asia, Latin America, Western Europe, and Central/Eastern Europe, supplemented by additional research published in the *Social Enterprise Journal*.

**Collaborative Framework**
The project was established through a partnership between the Interuniversity Attraction Pole on Social Enterprise (IAP-SOCENT) and the EMES International Research Network. Over its duration, it involved roughly 230 researchers and benefited from the support of COST Action 16206 Empower-SE.

**Methodology and Contribution**
Research was facilitated through localized partnerships, regional symposiums, and global meetings held alongside EMES International Conferences. Data collection was supported by a common international questionnaire.

**Institutional Acknowledgements**
The authors, Jacques Defourny and Marthe Nyssens, express gratitude to:
* Coordination and editorial staff: Sophie Adam and Elisabetta Severi.
* Financial partners: BELSPO, Fondation Crédit Coopératif, Groupe Caisse des Dépôts, and the InBev-Baillet Latour Fund.

This document provides biographical profiles of the editors and contributors involved in the International Comparative Social Enterprise Models (ICSEM) Project, spanning pages [S.10] through [S.18].

Key Points:

Management and Leadership
The project is scientifically coordinated by Jacques Defourny (HEC Liege) and Marthe Nyssens (UCLouvain). Both are central figures in the EMES International Research Network, which encompasses fourteen university research centers. The ICSEM Project itself represents a massive collaborative effort involving approximately 230 researchers from 55 countries.

Scope of Research
The contributors are experts in the third sector, social economy, and social entrepreneurship. Their collective research focuses on:
- Theoretical and conceptual approaches to social enterprises, non-profit organizations, and mutual societies.
- The interaction between these organizations, public policy, the market, and civil society.
- Socio-economic challenges, including work integration, rural development, social inclusion, and sustainable development.
- The application of these models across both industrialized and developing nations, with specific regional expertise in countries such as Russia, the Czech Republic, Slovakia, Hungary, Poland, Serbia, Albania, and Latvia.

Expertise and Background
The group consists of academics, policymakers, and practitioners who hold diverse roles, including university professors, research center directors, think tank leaders, and independent consultants. Many contributors possess extensive experience in multi-stakeholder process facilitation, project management, and institutional policy analysis.

Tribute
The text includes a formal tribute to the late Prof. Éva G. Fekete, noting her significant contributions to Hungarian rural development and her consistent advocacy for marginalized populations.

This section, titled "Introduction: Documenting, Theorising, Mapping and Testing the Plurality of SE Models in Central and Eastern Europe," identifies three primary factors that define the unique social enterprise (SE) landscape in Central and Eastern Europe (CEE):

Historical Legacy of State Socialism
The era of state socialism severely restricted or eliminated independent civil society organizations. This period left a lasting institutional and cultural impact that continues to influence how social enterprises are organized and how they interact with their environments today.

The "Transition Myth" and Neoliberal Influence
Economic and political development in the region has been heavily driven by a "transition myth" that prioritizes free-market mechanisms. This focus often marginalizes alternative economic models and collectives, as prevailing media and political narratives frequently overlook the importance of solidarity-based values.

The European Integration Narrative
Academic and policy discourse in the CEE region is heavily shaped by the pressure to "catch up" with established European Union standards. EU-led programs have played a decisive role in shaping the definition, mission, and homogenization of social enterprises across the region, often aligning them with broader EU development goals.

This text provides an introduction to the International Comparative Social Enterprise Models (ICSEM) Project, focusing on the diversity and development of social enterprise (SE) in Central and Eastern Europe (CEE).

### The Inherent Diversity of Social Enterprise
Researchers have long struggled to create a single, universally accepted definition for social enterprise. Because the field is highly diverse and context-dependent, recent scholarship—specifically the ICSEM Project—has shifted away from creating a single definition. Instead, it adopts an approach that acknowledges SE diversity and seeks to categorize different models based on their operational and institutional characteristics.

### Objectives of the ICSEM Project
The project was designed to address the lack of scientifically robust typologies for SE models. Its goals include:
1. Documenting the diversity of SE models across different geographical regions.
2. Integrating sound theoretical foundations (such as the EMES "ideal-typical" model) with large-scale empirical evidence.
3. Facilitating a better understanding of SE dynamics, ecosystems, and the institutional trajectories that shape them.

### Regional Findings: Central and Eastern Europe
The authors observe that the development of social enterprise in CEE countries is uniquely influenced by two primary factors:
- The Historical Legacy of State Socialism: The region’s history of socialist regimes suppressed cooperatives and civil society. The text distinguishes between former Soviet republics, "allied" socialist states, and the post-Yugoslav model, noting that the political and cultural atmosphere under these regimes left a lasting negative impact on the cooperative movement.
- External Influence: Unlike in Western Europe, where SE often emerges from bottom-up dynamics, SE in CEE is heavily linked to exogenous drivers. These include EU policies and international donor programs, which prioritize work integration for disadvantaged groups. Consequently, the SE concept in these countries is often narrowly defined, legally recognized but under-utilized, and suffers from "weak self-recognition," often serving more as an instrument for securing funding than as a reflection of deep organizational identity.

### Methodology
To ensure validity, the project employed a collaborative, multi-phase methodology. Researchers used a broad definition of social enterprise "made of organisations that combine an entrepreneurial dynamic to provide services or goods with the primacy of their social aims" (p. 25). By gathering country-specific contributions through large teams rather than individuals, the project avoided biased viewpoints and created a foundation for comparing institutional, legal, and operational frameworks globally.

The documentation provided covers pages 102 through 198, which constitute the core of Part I of the book. This section presents individual case studies detailing the historical roots, institutional frameworks, and development trends of social enterprises across ten specific countries in the Central and Eastern European (CEE) region.

Key points from pages 102 to 198:

Latvia (pp. 102-122): The text identifies specific challenges faced by social enterprises, focusing on the barriers to growth and the operational environment within the Latvian context.

Lithuania (pp. 123-137): This section explores the historical evolution of social ventures in Lithuania, connecting current trends to the nation's past institutional developments.

Poland (pp. 138-151): The authors examine the Polish social enterprise landscape through the lens of its unique institutional and historical background, providing a foundation for understanding its current state.

Romania (pp. 152-168): The chapter traces the institutionalization of social enterprise in Romania, highlighting critical historical milestones and the development of specific organizational models.

Russia (pp. 169-183): This analysis focuses on the efforts to cultivate social entrepreneurship models within the constraints and opportunities of the existing Russian legal and socio-economic frameworks.

Slovak Republic (pp. 184-198): The text offers an overview of the landscape for social enterprises in Slovakia, mapping the current state of the sector.

The overarching theme throughout these chapters is the effort by researchers to capture the "plurality of SE models." By analyzing these diverse national experiences, the authors seek to avoid a single, forced definition of social enterprise, instead emphasizing how local contexts and institutional legacies shape how these organizations attempt to balance market-based income with the pursuit of social aims.

The section covering pages 199–217 focuses on comparative analyses and perspectives regarding the social enterprise sector in Central and Eastern Europe (CEE). The core objective is to move beyond individual national reports to understand the regional landscape through broader research themes.

Historical and Institutional Legacies
The primary focus of this section is on how past institutional and historical contexts shape contemporary social enterprises. By examining case studies from Poland, Hungary, the Czech Republic, and Croatia, the researchers highlight that social enterprises do not emerge in a vacuum. Instead, they are deeply influenced by the specific political, social, and economic transitions these nations have navigated. 

Key Findings:
The development of social enterprise models in CEE countries is highly dependent on institutional path dependencies.
There is a clear trend toward documenting, theorizing, and mapping the plurality of social enterprise models rather than attempting to force a single, unified definition.
The research underscores that "bottom-up" approaches are essential for identifying the major social enterprise models that function within the diverse realities of the CEE region.
The interplay between established historical frameworks and new socio-economic developments creates a unique ecosystem that informs how social enterprises seek to balance their entrepreneurial spirit with a primary commitment to societal aims.

Methodological Approach
The analysis presented in these pages relies on the International Comparative Social Enterprise Models (ICSEM) project’s strategy. This approach promotes the idea that the field gains more value from acknowledging local variations and "locally embedded approaches" than from creating an overly ambitious, encompassing definition. By recognizing the legitimacy of these local models, the researchers establish common ground for understanding the development of social entrepreneurship on an international scale.

The section covered by pages 218–272 focuses on comparative analyses and thematic perspectives regarding social enterprises in Central and Eastern Europe (CEE). The key points are structured as follows:

Role of External Financing (pp. 218–234)
This chapter examines how social entrepreneurs in the CEE region secure resources. It highlights that reliance on external funding—such as state support, European Union grants, or private philanthropy—is a critical factor in the development of these organizations. The research explores the advantages and potential risks of this dependency on the sustainability and autonomy of social enterprises.

Endogenous vs. Exogenous Drivers (pp. 235–251)
This study compares the factors that stimulate the growth of the social enterprise sector. Endogenous drivers refer to internal grassroots initiatives, community needs, and local entrepreneurship, while exogenous drivers involve external pressures or incentives, such as international policy frameworks and top-down institutional mandates. The authors analyze how the interplay between these two forces shapes the sector's evolution.

Social Enterprises in Rural Areas (pp. 252–272)
Focusing on the Baltic States, this comparative study explores the unique challenges and opportunities for social enterprises in rural contexts. It discusses how these organizations often serve as essential drivers for local development, providing social services and employment opportunities where conventional market actors may be absent. The text emphasizes the importance of community involvement and the adaptation of business models to fit specific rural socioeconomic landscapes.

The provided document does not contain pages 273 through 327. The text concludes after page 291, which serves as the index. Based on the available content, I can summarize the core objectives and methodology surrounding the ICSEM project as discussed in the text:

Research Strategy and Objectives
The ICSEM project moves away from the pursuit of a single, encompassing definition of social enterprise, which the editors view as an "impossibility." Instead, the project employs a bottom-up research strategy designed to capture the diversity of social enterprises within their specific local and national contexts. This approach provides legitimacy to locally embedded models while allowing for the identification of common international characteristics.

Core Definitions
Social enterprises are defined by the combination of an entrepreneurial spirit with the prioritization of social or societal goals. These organizations typically fund their operations through a mix of market income generated from goods or services and other resource types. Additionally, the project emphasizes "social innovation," defined as the implementation of new ideas aimed at creating fairer and more sustainable societal changes.

Project Scope and Regional Focus
The book focusing on Central and Eastern Europe is the final volume in a four-part series covering Asia, Latin America, Western Europe, and Central and Eastern Europe. The research was conducted over eight years by approximately 230 researchers from 55 countries, supported by the Belgian Science Policy (BELSPO) and the COST Action 16206 "Empower-SE" network.

Key Themes and Structure
The work aims to serve as a reference for students, policymakers, and researchers by:
1. Providing national overviews of social enterprise landscapes (Chapters 1–11).
2. Conducting comparative analyses on historical legacies, institutionalization, external financing, and the role of endogenous versus exogenous drivers (Chapters 12–15).
3. Evaluating the relevance of various social enterprise models within the specific socio-economic contexts of Central and Eastern Europe (Chapter 16).
Here is a quiz for this lesson.

questions.question: What was the primary research motivation for the International Comparative Social Enterprise Models (ICSEM) Project?
options: To establish a single, globally applicable definition of social enterprise.
To prioritize theoretical debates over the analysis of diverse practical models.
To embrace diversity by using bottom-up approaches rather than seeking one unified definition.
To focus exclusively on the economic performance metrics of Western European enterprises.
questions.explanation: The text states the project operated on the hypothesis of 'the impossibility of a unified definition', opting instead for bottom-up approaches to capture local and national diversity.

questions.question: According to the provided text, what is the core characteristic that distinguishes social enterprises?
options: The complete absence of market income to maintain non-profit status.
The combination of an entrepreneurial spirit with the prioritization of social or societal aims.
The exclusive use of public funding to implement social innovation.
The obligation to operate only within the civil society sector without forming partnerships.
questions.explanation: The text defines social enterprises as entities that 'seek to combine an entrepreneurial spirit and behaviour with a primacy of social or societal aims.'

questions.question: How did the ICSEM project utilize local research partners?
options: They were primarily tasked with translating Western European models to other regions.
They served only as administrative support for the Belgian research team.
They were relied upon for their commitment and efforts in documenting and analyzing local social enterprise ecosystems.
They were discouraged from considering historical or institutional legacies during their data collection.
questions.explanation: The text credits the production of the volumes to the 'efforts and commitment of local ICSEM Research Partners' in documenting and analyzing the diversity of social enterprise models.

questions.question: What does the text identify as a 'social innovation'?
options: The maximization of profit before distributing it to shareholders.
The implementation of a new idea or initiative to change society in a fairer and more sustainable direction.
The act of securing government grants to replace market income.
The replacement of conventional businesses with strictly non-profit entities.
questions.explanation: The content explicitly defines a social innovation as 'the implementation of a new idea or initiative to change society in a fairer and more sustainable direction.'

questions.question: Why is the collaboration with COST Action 16206 (Empower-SE) significant for this research?
options: It focused exclusively on the financial auditing of social enterprises.
It provided funding for infrastructure projects in Central and Eastern Europe.
It helped connect research initiatives across Europe and enabled scientists to grow their ideas by sharing them.
It acted as the sole author of the book series, replacing academic researchers.
questions.explanation: The text notes that COST (European Cooperation in Science and Technology) helps 'connect research initiatives across Europe and enable scientists to grow their ideas by sharing them with their peers.'

cards.front: What was the main research objective of the ICSEM Project?
cards.back: To document and analyze the diversity of social enterprise models across different local and national contexts rather than attempting to provide one unified definition.

cards.front: What does a social enterprise typically combine according to the text?
cards.back: An entrepreneurial spirit and behavior with a primacy of social or societal goals, often using market income alongside other resource types.

cards.front: How is a 'social innovation' defined in this context?
cards.back: The implementation of a new idea or initiative intended to change society in a fairer and more sustainable direction.

cards.front: What research strategy did the ICSEM project adopt?
cards.back: A bottom-up approach to capture the social enterprise phenomenon within specific local and national contexts.

cards.front: Who edited the book 'Social Enterprise in Central and Eastern Europe'?
What does the COST Action 16206 'Empower-SE' support?
cards.back: Research initiatives across Europe, helping connect scholars and scientists to boost innovation and share knowledge.

cards.front: According to the ICSEM project literature, why is a 'unified definition' of social enterprise considered problematic?
cards.back: Because the field is characterized by a huge diversity of initiatives; a unified definition fails to account for locally embedded approaches.

cards.front: What are the four world regions covered by the series of ICSEM-based books?
cards.back: Asia, Latin America, Western Europe, and Central and Eastern Europe.

cards.front: Which research network did Jacques Defourny and Marthe Nyssens both serve as President of?
What is the primary focus of Routledge Studies in Social Enterprise & Social Innovation as a series?
cards.back: To examine participatory governance, social innovation dynamics, and partnerships between the third sector, civil society, businesses, and public authorities.

slides.title: Social Enterprise in Central and Eastern Europe
bullets: Explores social enterprise development in the CEE region.
Part of the global ICSEM Project research series.
slides.notes: This presentation introduces the comprehensive study of social enterprise models across Central and Eastern Europe. It leverages the work of the ICSEM Project to understand this diverse field.

slides.title: Research Strategy: Embracing Diversity
bullets: Rejects a single, unified definition of social enterprise.
Utilizes bottom-up approaches in local and national contexts.
Identifies major models to establish common ground.
Prioritizes local diversity over idealistic encompassing definitions.
slides.notes: Instead of forcing one definition, researchers focused on identifying diverse models empirically, allowing for a more accurate reflection of the reality on the ground.

slides.title: Defining Social Enterprise
bullets: Combines entrepreneurial spirit with primary social aims.
Uses market income alongside other resource types.
Promotes social innovation for fairer, sustainable change.
Focuses on participatory governance and societal dynamics.
slides.notes: Social enterprises are characterized by their dual commitment to social goals and entrepreneurial methods, often involving diverse revenue streams and community-oriented management.

slides.title: The Specific Context of CEE
bullets: Legacy of state socialism limited organizational development.
Dominance of 'transition' economic myths favors free markets.
European integration narratives drive local policy agendas.
Sector growth relies heavily on international donor funding.
slides.notes: The unique historical and political backdrop of the CEE region has significantly shaped how social enterprises emerge, often contrasting with trends in Western Europe.

slides.title: The ICSEM Project Methodology
bullets: Documented diversity across 55 countries through global partners.
Rooted in sound theoretical grounds and empirical evidence.
Exploits large international datasets to test SE models.
Bridges the gap between grassroots dynamics and policy.
slides.notes: The research spanned several years, gathering hundreds of scholars to systematically analyze social enterprise, ensuring models are tested against empirical data.
